title: Classic Upholstered Chair
summary: A simple accent chair with a 2x4 back-leg frame, an angled seat back, and a padded seat you staple and upholster yourself.
description:
Getting both back legs to match is the entire skill in this build, and the source's own advice is worth taking literally: practice the leg cut on a scrap piece of 2x4 before committing to the real stock. Cut as much as possible with a circular saw, finishing only the interior curves with a jigsaw, then lay the two finished legs together and check that the top, bottom, and front edges all line up — those are the surfaces that show and bear load, so they're the ones worth double-checking; the rest matters far less.

The upholstery is a straightforward staple-and-wrap job, not sewing: spray-glue the foam to the plywood seat top, lay fabric face-down with the foam-side seat on top, then staple the fabric to the underside, tucking corners neatly as you go. A chair like this is a genuinely inexpensive build — the source's own version came in under $20 in materials.

tools.note: Finishes the interior curves on the back legs.

cutList.note: Practice on scrap first; front cut inset 1/4" for rounded 2x4 edges.
Both ends cut at 45°, not parallel to each other.
Longest point measurement, one end cut at 8° off square.

steps.title: Cut the back legs
steps.body: Mark and cut the back legs, insetting the front cut (where the leg connects to the side aprons) by 1/4" to account for the rounded edges on 2x4 stock. Make as many cuts as possible with a circular saw, finishing the interior cuts on the back with a jigsaw. Practice the cut on a scrap piece of 2x4 first. Once cut, lay both legs together and check that the top, bottom, and front sides all match — the other edges matter less. Sand the cuts smooth.

steps.title: Cut the rest of the parts
steps.body: From the 1x4, cut two side aprons 17 1/2" long and two front and back aprons 16" long — that is 67" of the 72" board, so cut the long pieces first. From the 2x2, cut two front legs 17 1/4" and four corner braces 4" long with both ends at 45° and not parallel to each other. From the 1x2, cut the seat back top 16", two base side supports 19" (longest-point measurement, one end cut 8° off square so it can follow the rake of the back leg), and the base center support 16 3/4". From the 1x6, cut two seat back boards 16" long, and cut the plywood seat top panel to 19" x 19". Label the two 19" base supports as you cut them so the angled end does not get squared off by mistake later.

steps.title: Attach the side aprons
steps.body: Stand the two back legs on edge with their front cuts facing each other and set a 17 1/2" side apron against each leg, flush to the bottom of that front cut and flush to the leg's outside face. Fasten with pocket-hole screws driven from the inside of the apron into the leg, or predrill and screw through the leg — either way put the fasteners where the seat and upholstery will hide them. Before the glue sets, lay the two leg-and-apron assemblies on top of one another and check that they still match at the top, bottom, and front edges. That match is the one error you cannot correct later.

steps.title: Attach the front legs
steps.body: Set a 17 1/4" front leg at the front end of each side apron with the leg's top flush to the apron's top edge, so the two together give one flat surface for the seat frame. Glue and screw through the apron into the leg, predrilling first — 2x2 pine splits easily when a screw lands near a corner. Stand the two side assemblies up and sight across them: the front legs should be parallel and the tops of all four legs should land in one plane.

steps.title: Attach the front and back aprons
steps.body: Join the two side assemblies with the 16" front and back aprons, tops flush with the tops of the side aprons. That 16" is what sets the chair's width: 16" between the two 1 1/2" front legs comes to 19" overall, matching the 19" x 19" plywood seat panel, so measure across the front before you drive anything. Use pocket holes drilled into the ends of each apron plus glue, and predrill if you screw through a face instead — 1x4 pine splits readily at the end grain. Check both diagonals across the seat frame and adjust until they match.

steps.title: Attach the seat back top
steps.body: The 16" seat back top is the 1x2 that caps the two back legs and ties them together — the same 16" span the back apron uses. Set it across the legs, glue it, and drive two screws per end into predrilled holes so the 3/4" stock does not split. Measure from leg to leg at the seat and again up at the top: equal readings mean the back is not racked, and this is the last easy chance to correct it.

steps.title: Attach the seat back
steps.body: Fill the opening between the back legs with the two 16" 1x6 seat back boards, leaving a small even gap between them and below the seat back top so the back reads as slats rather than one solid panel. Fasten each board with pocket holes drilled from the back face, or predrill through the legs and screw in from outside. Because the back legs rake backwards, the boards sit on that rake — hold each one in place and mark it against the legs rather than measuring up from the floor.

steps.title: Attach the base supports
steps.body: The two 19" base side supports run the full length of the seat frame and the shorter base center support fits between them. Set each 19" support inside the frame with its 8° end against the raked back leg and its square end at the front, top edge level and low enough that the 1/2" plywood seat will finish flush with the tops of the aprons. Glue and screw them into the aprons and legs. These supports carry the sitter's weight, so they add real strength — they are not just a ledge for the seat to rest on.

steps.title: Attach the base center support
steps.body: Fit the base center support between the two side supports, midway front to back, and fasten with glue and a screw into each end. Measure the actual gap between your installed side supports before trimming it: 16 3/4" plus the two 3/4" supports comes to 18 1/4" rather than the frame's 19", so the listed length does not close on its own. Then glue and screw the four 4" corner braces into the inside corners of the apron frame, each brace's 45° ends bearing flat against the two aprons it spans. The braces are what stop the chair racking when someone leans back in it.

steps.title: Upholster the seat
steps.body: Spray-glue the 19" x 19" x 1" foam to the 19" x 19" plywood seat top with the edges aligned. Lay the fabric face-down on a table, centre the seat on it foam-side down, and pull the fabric up over the edges, stapling to the underside of the plywood with 3/8" staples — work the middle of each side first, then come back and fold each corner neatly instead of bunching it. Trim the excess so it will not foul the frame, then drop the seat onto the base supports and fasten from underneath through the supports, choosing a screw short enough that it cannot break through the 1/2" plywood and the foam above it.

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand every exposed wood surface through the assorted grits and ease the sharp edges on the legs, aprons and back where hands and clothes will catch. Vacuum and wipe the dust off before finishing. Paint or stain as you like, and put the finish on the bottoms of the legs too — that is the surface that gets dragged across a floor.

images.alt: Classic upholstered accent chair with 2x4 back legs
